Their custom procurement system (Compass) has been retired, leaving a critical gap in the sourcing middle — Oracle handles PRs and warehouse/finance but has no mid-process sourcing capability (RFQ creation, bid management, evaluation, awarding). All procurement is currently managed via email and Excel. A new major railway infrastructure project has created an urgent, contractually-mandated requirement for automated end-to-end procurement reporting from PR through to site delivery IMPACT/PAIN (1) 100% of RFQs and bid submissions are email-based with no audit trail, no structure, and no digital evaluation capability; (2) Manual procurement reporting is failing a client contractual requirement on the railway project — putting Orascom at legal/contractual risk; (3) Multi-level approval workflows (PM → Director → BU Director → Branch → COO) are unmanaged and undocumented; (4) No visibility over procurement status across complex international projects with multiple logistics milestones (fabrication, shipping, customs, site delivery) PROPOSED RESOLUTION/STRATEGY Phase 1 (Urgent): Deploy Zycus eSourcing + Intake Management integrated with Oracle to bridge the PR→RFQ→award→PO gap for the railway project within 3 months. Phase 2: Full S2P deployment including contract management, supplier management, spend analysis. Oracle integration as the backbone throughout BD: Next step is brief 2nd round of demonstrtaions with broader team BD: Intorductiry call done on May 14th and Demo was done on May 19th they is an clear interest in eprocurement + Sourcing

metrics.narrative: Pain is confirmed and articulated across four operational/financial dimensions per 5 May discovery call, but no quantified ROI, cost savings, cycle-time improvement, or error-rate reduction has been stated by the buyer. (1) 100% of RFQs and bid submissions are email-based with no audit trail, no structure, and no digital evaluation capability. (2) Manual procurement reporting is failing a client contractual requirement on the railway project, putting Orascom at legal/contractual risk (contractual penalty or project jeopardy not quantified). (3) Multi-level approval workflows (PM to Director to the relevant stakeholder to Branch to COO) are unmanaged and undocumented, creating compliance and cycle-time risk (duration or error rate not quantified). (4) No visibility over procurement status across complex international projects with multiple logistics milestones (fabrication, shipping, customs, site delivery), creating project-delivery and client-reporting risk (impact not quantified). The railway project contractual mandate for automated end-to-end procurement reporting is the clearest forcing function and pain, but the financial or operational penalty for non-compliance was not quantified on the 5 May call or in any subsequent conversation. Quantifying the railway project contractual penalty, procurement cycle-time cost (days/FTE per RFQ), and audit/compliance exposure is the next move to build a defendable business case and secure executive sponsorship.

champion.narrative: No champion identified or developed. A champion is an internal advocate with access to the Economic Buyer or Decision Maker who actively drives the decision forward, introduces Zycus to other stakeholders, shares internal intel, and pushes back on competitors or inaction on Zycus's behalf. None of the four engaged contacts meet this bar. Tarek Ibrahim Yousef (Director of Procurement, Evaluator role) and Michael Toma (Procurement Team Leader, Decision Maker role in SF but procurement function, not commercial authority) were the primary contacts on the 5 May discovery, 14 May workshop, and 19 May demo, but their engagement has been passive: they attended scheduled Zycus-led sessions, requested information (budgetary estimate, pricing tables), but have not introduced Zycus to executive sponsors, advocated for urgency, or driven next steps. The 32-day silence from 2 June (rep's in-person meeting request) to 2 July (buyer's workshop request citing solution clarity concerns) and the confidence drop to 10% indicate the procurement team lacks authority, urgency, or conviction to move the deal forward. George Georgy Luke (Procurement Engineer/Team Leader, Influencer) and Mohammed Kefaya (Procurement, Parsons contractor) have even lighter engagement. No contact has demonstrated advocacy, executive access, or active deal-driving behavior. The 2 July workshop request is reactive (seeking clarification, not driving progression) and signals confusion or concern rather than championship. Developing a champion requires identifying a procurement or project-delivery leader with executive access and railway-project accountability, equipping them with a quantified business case and executive-ready ROI narrative, and securing their active advocacy and internal sponsorship. Without a champion the deal will stall in procurement evaluation indefinitely.

competition.narrative: Three competitors confirmed, with Oracle as the primary threat due to incumbent ERP integration leverage and switching-cost advantage. (1) Oracle (incumbent ERP, sourcing module evaluated and declined): Oracle handles PRs, POs, warehouse, and finance and is deeply integrated into Orascom's core procurement and financial systems. Orascom evaluated Oracle's sourcing module and declined it on or before 5 May 2026 due to 'lack of detailed RFQ-to-PO visibility' per the discovery call, but Oracle is not eliminated. Oracle is the path of least resistance: if Oracle closes the product gap (future module release, third-party bolt-on, or bundled discount) during Zycus's 32-day silence, Oracle can win on integration simplicity, lower TCO, and single-vendor convenience. Oracle is the primary competitive risk. (2) Do-nothing / email and Excel: the status quo (100% email-based RFQs, manual reporting) is a competitor in the sense that it is the current state and the buyer may decide the pain is tolerable or the Zycus solution too complex/expensive/slow. The 2 July workshop request citing solution clarity concerns and the 10% confidence rating suggest the buyer is questioning whether Zycus is worth the effort and timeline. (3) Inaction / timeline risk: the railway project procurement starts August–September 2026 but Zycus Phase 1 go-live is realistically Q1 2027 (6–8 months from contract signature). If the buyer concludes Zycus cannot deliver in time, they may defer the purchase, rely on manual workarounds for the railway project, and re-evaluate in 2027, effectively losing to inaction. The wedge against Oracle is superior RFQ-to-PO sourcing visibility and workflow depth, which Oracle's module lacked per 5 May. The wedge must be demonstrated and reinforced with construction-specific proof (Balfour Beatty, Shimizu references offered 19 May demo) and a clear Oracle integration story. The activation is to quantify the railway project contractual risk, secure executive sponsorship, and fast-track Phase 1 scoping to compress the timeline gap. The 32-day silence and 2 July buyer confusion signal Oracle or inaction may be winning by default.

sources: SF Next_Step__c 5 May 2026 SITUATION/IMPACT/PAIN (discovery call summary)
SF Next_Step__c 5 May 'contractually-mandated requirement for automated end-to-end procurement reporting from PR through to site delivery' (railway project forcing event)
identify_pain.narrative: Pain is confirmed, articulated, and anchored to a contractual forcing event, but the pain owner (the executive accountable for the railway project contractual compliance and the procurement transformation) is not identified. Four pain dimensions were articulated on the 5 May discovery call: (1) Email-and-Excel procurement chaos: 100% of RFQs and bid submissions are email-based with no audit trail, no structure, and no digital evaluation capability, creating compliance, cycle-time, and decision-quality risk. (2) Contractual jeopardy on the railway project: manual procurement reporting is failing a client contractual requirement, putting Orascom at legal/contractual risk. This is the most acute and time-bound pain, with procurement starting August–September 2026. (3) Unmanaged approval workflows: multi-level approval chains (PM to Director to the relevant stakeholder to Branch to COO) are undocumented and unmanaged, creating compliance, audit, and delay risk. (4) Zero logistics visibility: no visibility over procurement status across complex international projects with multiple logistics milestones (fabrication, shipping, customs, site delivery), creating project-delivery risk and client-reporting failure. The railway project contractual mandate is the primary pain and the forcing function for urgency, but the buyer has not quantified the penalty for non-compliance or named the executive accountable for the railway project delivery who would own this pain. Tarek Ibrahim Yousef (Director of Procurement) and Michael Toma (Procurement Team Leader) articulated the pain on 5 May but are operational executors, not the pain owner. Identifying the Branch Director, BU Director, or COO accountable for the railway project and making them the pain owner is the next move to create executive urgency.

SF Description field (not read, may contain contract/legal detail if populated)
paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics, legal review process, procurement channel (direct vs. through SI/partner), and InfoSec/IT approval requirements are entirely unknown. No discussion of MSA, SOW, redlining process, legal review timeline, or InfoSec/data-security approval has been logged in any call summary or Next Step entry. The 5 May discovery call proposed a two-phase deployment (Phase 1 urgent eSourcing/Intake/Oracle integration for railway project within 3 months, Phase 2 full S2P including contract management, supplier management, spend analytics) but did not address contract structure, payment terms, implementation SOW, or legal/InfoSec gates required before kickoff. The railway project's August–September 2026 procurement start is a forcing timeline but no corresponding contract-execution or legal-approval timeline has been mapped. Orascom is a large international EPC contractor operating across MENA, Africa, and the US, implying multi-entity contracting complexity, but entity structure, signatory authority, and cross-border data/hosting requirements have not been explored. The absence of any paper-process discussion 52 days into Qualified, with 181 days to a December close and a 6–8 month implementation window, is a critical gap. Mapping the contract approval chain, legal review timeline, required data-security/InfoSec approvals, and SI/partner involvement (if any) in the next workshop or executive meeting is essential to validate the timeline and advance the deal.

economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer identified or engaged in 52 days. The buyer articulated on 5 May that PO approvals follow a multi-level chain (PM to Director to the relevant stakeholder to Branch to COO) depending on amount, and for a circa $200k platform purchase C-suite sign-off is required. Yet no executive has been introduced, invited to a call, or engaged in any forum. All four contact roles are procurement-function mid-level: Tarek Ibrahim Yousef (Director of Procurement, Evaluator), George Georgy Luke (Procurement Engineer/Team Leader, Influencer), Michael Toma (Procurement Team Leader, Decision Maker role in SF but procurement-function title, not commercial authority), Mohammed Kefaya (Procurement, Parsons contractor). No CFO, COO, CIO, or Branch Director appears in any contact role, task, or call. The 32-day post-demo silence (2 June to 2 July) and confidence drop to 10% suggest the procurement team lacks executive backing or authority to move the purchase forward. Without EB engagement the deal cannot advance from Qualified, and the $200k forecast is indefensible. Identifying and engaging the COO (named in the approval chain) or a Branch/BU Director with budget authority is the critical next move.

decision_process.narrative: The decision process has been articulated at a high level but not validated or advanced in 52 days. Per 5 May discovery call, Orascom follows a multi-level approval workflow: PM to Director to the relevant stakeholder to Branch to COO, with COO sign-off required for purchases of this magnitude ($200k). The call sequence to date shows procurement-function-only engagement: 5 May discovery (Tarek, Michael Toma, George implied), 14 May workshop (procurement team), 19 May demo (procurement team and 'key stakeholders' per Next Step but no names or titles logged). No executive, finance, legal, IT/integration, or project-delivery stakeholder has joined a call or been introduced. The 25 June proposal share and 2 June in-person meeting request both targeted the same procurement contacts with no escalation. The process has stalled at the procurement evaluation layer with no upward movement toward the BU Director, Branch, or COO approval gates described 5 May. The 2 July workshop request suggests the procurement team is circling back to foundational solution-fit questions rather than advancing toward commercial or executive gates. The decision process is known in outline but not being executed, signaling either lack of procurement-team authority, lack of executive sponsorship, or both. Escalating to a Branch or BU Director and mapping the approval timeline (who signs when, what documents/approvals are required at each gate) is the next move to unlock progression.

decision_criteria.narrative: Three explicit decision criteria emerged from the 5 May discovery and 19 May demo, confirmed by the buyer's focus and the 2 July workshop request, but formal evaluation scorecard, RFP criteria, or weighted decision matrix have not been shared. (1) End-to-end procurement reporting capability: the railway project contractual mandate requires automated reporting from PR creation through to site delivery in one consolidated report. This is the primary forcing criterion. The buyer asked on 19 May for a budgetary estimate and reviewed the commercial tables, signaling they are evaluating cost alongside capability. (2) Logistics milestone tracking without a dedicated logistics module: the buyer requires visibility over fabrication start, FAT inspection, shipping, port arrival, customs, and site delivery. Zycus proposed a workaround using custom fields in sourcing and contract modules but has not demonstrated or prototyped it. The 2 July workshop request citing 'clarifications related to mean modules' and 'solution not clear with the business use cases' suggests this workaround is under-explained or unconvincing. (3) Oracle integration depth: Oracle is the incumbent ERP handling PRs, POs, warehouse, and finance. Seamless integration is a stated requirement per 5 May 'Oracle integration as the backbone throughout.' The extent of integration effort, data mapping, and dual-system workflow has not been detailed or validated. The buyer's 2 July request for a workshop to clarify 'mean modules' and business-use-case fit indicates confusion or concern that the proposed solution does not map cleanly to their stated criteria. Formalizing these criteria in a shared evaluation framework and demonstrating the logistics-milestone workaround in the 2 July workshop are the next moves.

SITUATION

Orascom Construction is a large international EPC/construction contractor operating across MENA, Africa, and the US. Their custom procurement system (Compass) has been retired, leaving a critical gap in the sourcing middle — Oracle handles PRs and warehouse/finance but has no mid-process sourcing capability (RFQ creation, bid management, evaluation, awarding). All procurement is currently managed via email and Excel. A new major railway infrastructure project has created an urgent, contractually-mandated requirement for automated end-to-end procurement reporting from PR through to site delivery

IMPACT/PAIN

(1) 100% of RFQs and bid submissions are email-based with no audit trail, no structure, and no digital evaluation capability; (2) Manual procurement reporting is failing a client contractual requirement on the railway project — putting Orascom at legal/contractual risk; (3) Multi-level approval workflows (PM → Director → BU Director → Branch → COO) are unmanaged and undocumented; (4) No visibility over procurement status across complex international projects with multiple logistics milestones (fabrication, shipping, customs, site delivery)

PROPOSED RESOLUTION/STRATEGY

Phase 1 (Urgent): Deploy Zycus eSourcing + Intake Management integrated with Oracle to bridge the PR→RFQ→award→PO gap for the railway project within 3 months. Phase 2: Full S2P deployment including contract management, supplier management, spend analysis. Oracle integration as the backbone throughout

SITUATION

Orascom Construction is a large international EPC/construction contractor operating across MENA, Africa, and the US. Their custom procurement system (Compass) has been retired, leaving a critical gap in the sourcing middle — Oracle handles PRs and warehouse/finance but has no mid-process sourcing capability (RFQ creation, bid management, evaluation, awarding). All procurement is currently managed via email and Excel. A new major railway infrastructure project has created an urgent, contractually-mandated requirement for automated end-to-end procurement reporting from PR through to site delivery

IMPACT/PAIN

(1) 100% of RFQs and bid submissions are email-based with no audit trail, no structure, and no digital evaluation capability; (2) Manual procurement reporting is failing a client contractual requirement on the railway project — putting Orascom at legal/contractual risk; (3) Multi-level approval workflows (PM → Director → BU Director → Branch → COO) are unmanaged and undocumented; (4) No visibility over procurement status across complex international projects with multiple logistics milestones (fabrication, shipping, customs, site delivery)

PROPOSED RESOLUTION/STRATEGY

Phase 1 (Urgent): Deploy Zycus eSourcing + Intake Management integrated with Oracle to bridge the PR→RFQ→award→PO gap for the railway project within 3 months.
